Outcomes.Text: Explain the principles of supervised machine learning and how regression and classification models are applied to engineering problems.
Apply scikit-learn workflows to train, evaluate, and interpret machine learning models for engineering datasets.
Create engineering automation pipelines and parameter sweep scripts to systematically explore design or model parameters.
Demonstrate proficiency in Git and GitHub version control practices including committing, branching, and collaborating on code repositories.
Identify the risks associated with AI-generated code and apply verification strategies to ensure correctness and safety.
Evaluate professional engineering accountability obligations when integrating AI tools into technical workflows.

Topics.Title: Foundations of Supervised Machine Learning
Topics.Summary: Introduces the core concepts of supervised learning, distinguishing between regression and classification tasks and explaining how labeled training data drives model development.

Elements.Title: What Is Supervised Learning?
Elements.BodyText: Supervised learning is a machine learning paradigm in which a model is trained on labeled input-output pairs to learn a mapping from features to targets.
Elements.Notes: Contrast with unsupervised and reinforcement learning to help learners place supervised learning in the broader ML landscape.

Items.Text: The algorithm learns by minimizing the error between its predictions and the known labels in the training data.
Labeled datasets are the essential ingredient that distinguishes supervised learning from other paradigms.
The trained model can then generalize to make predictions on new, unseen data.

Elements.Title: Regression vs. Classification
Elements.BodyText: Regression predicts a continuous numerical output, while classification predicts a discrete category or class label.
Elements.Notes: Engineering examples help ground the distinction: predicting beam deflection is regression; predicting whether a material will fracture under load is classification.

Items.Text: Regression models output a numeric value such as temperature, stress, or yield strength.
Classification models assign inputs to one of two or more predefined categories.
The choice between regression and classification is determined by the nature of the target variable in the dataset.
Both task types share the same fundamental training loop but use different loss functions and evaluation metrics.

Elements.Title: Features, Labels, and Training Data
Elements.BodyText: Features are the measurable input variables used to make predictions, while labels are the corresponding known outputs used during training.
Elements.Notes: Emphasize data quality: noisy, insufficient, or unrepresentative training data is the most common cause of poor model performance in engineering applications.

Items.Text: Feature engineering—selecting and transforming raw measurements—significantly affects model accuracy.
A well-constructed training dataset must be representative of the conditions the model will encounter in deployment.
Splitting data into training and test sets prevents overfitting and provides an honest estimate of model performance.

Elements.Title: Bias-Variance Trade-off
Elements.BodyText: The bias-variance trade-off describes the tension between a model that is too simple (high bias) and one that is too complex (high variance).
Elements.Notes: Use a learning curve or validation curve plot to visualize underfitting and overfitting in practice.

Items.Text: High bias leads to underfitting, where the model fails to capture the underlying pattern in the data.
High variance leads to overfitting, where the model memorizes training data but performs poorly on new data.
Cross-validation is the standard technique for detecting and managing the bias-variance trade-off.
Regularization techniques can reduce variance by penalizing model complexity.

Elements.Title: Model Evaluation Metrics
Elements.BodyText: Selecting appropriate evaluation metrics is essential for understanding how well a model performs on the intended engineering task.
Elements.Notes: For regression, common metrics include MAE, MSE, RMSE, and R-squared. For classification, accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices are standard.

Items.Text: Mean Absolute Error and Root Mean Squared Error quantify average prediction error in regression tasks.
R-squared indicates the proportion of variance in the target that is explained by the model.
Accuracy alone can be misleading in imbalanced classification problems; precision and recall provide more insight.
A confusion matrix visualizes true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for classifiers.

Topics.Title: Machine Learning with scikit-learn
Topics.Summary: Covers the practical use of the scikit-learn library to build, train, and evaluate regression and classification models in engineering contexts.

Elements.Title: The scikit-learn API Design
Elements.BodyText: scikit-learn provides a consistent estimator API in which every model object exposes fit, predict, and score methods, making it straightforward to swap algorithms.
Elements.Notes: The uniformity of the API is one of scikit-learn's greatest strengths—once learners understand the pattern for one model, they can apply it to any other.

Items.Text: The fit method trains the model on provided feature and label arrays.
The predict method generates output predictions for new input data.
The score method returns a default performance metric appropriate to the estimator type.
Consistent interfaces allow rapid experimentation by substituting different algorithm classes with minimal code changes.

Elements.Title: Data Preprocessing and Pipelines
Elements.BodyText: Raw engineering data often requires scaling, encoding, or imputation before it can be fed into a machine learning model, and scikit-learn pipelines chain these steps with the estimator.
Elements.Notes: Stress that preprocessing steps must be fit only on training data and then applied to test data to avoid data leakage, which inflates apparent model performance.

Items.Text: StandardScaler normalizes features to zero mean and unit variance, which benefits gradient-based and distance-based algorithms.
Categorical variables must be encoded numerically using techniques such as one-hot encoding before most models can process them.
sklearn.pipeline.Pipeline chains preprocessing transformers and the final estimator into a single object.
Using a pipeline ensures that cross-validation correctly applies preprocessing within each fold.

Elements.Title: Regression Models in scikit-learn
Elements.BodyText: scikit-learn offers a range of regression estimators suitable for engineering data, from linear models to ensemble methods.
Elements.Notes: LinearRegression is a useful baseline. RandomForestRegressor and GradientBoostingRegressor often provide better accuracy on nonlinear engineering relationships but are less interpretable.

Items.Text: LinearRegression fits a hyperplane to minimize the sum of squared residuals and is interpretable through its coefficients.
Ridge and Lasso regression add regularization terms to combat overfitting and can perform feature selection.
Ensemble regressors such as Random Forest combine many decision trees to improve predictive accuracy on complex data.
Comparing multiple regression models on a held-out test set is best practice before selecting a final model.

Elements.Title: Classification Models in scikit-learn
Elements.BodyText: Classification estimators in scikit-learn assign input samples to discrete classes and are evaluated using metrics such as accuracy, precision, and recall.
Elements.Notes: Logistic Regression, despite its name, is a linear classifier and is a strong baseline for binary engineering classification problems.

Items.Text: LogisticRegression models the probability that a sample belongs to each class using a logistic function.
Decision tree classifiers produce interpretable rules that can be visualized, aiding engineering insight.
Support Vector Machines find the maximum-margin hyperplane separating classes and work well in high-dimensional spaces.
Class imbalance in engineering fault-detection datasets may require oversampling, undersampling, or adjusted class weights.

Elements.Title: Hyperparameter Tuning
Elements.BodyText: Hyperparameters are model configuration settings that are not learned from data and must be set before training; tuning them improves model performance.
Elements.Notes: GridSearchCV exhaustively searches a specified parameter grid; RandomizedSearchCV is more efficient for large search spaces.

Items.Text: Cross-validated grid search evaluates all combinations of specified hyperparameter values and selects the best-performing set.
Randomized search samples parameter combinations probabilistically and is preferred when the search space is large.
Hyperparameter tuning should always be performed on training or validation data, never on the final test set.
Documenting the tuning process and results is important for reproducibility and engineering records.

Elements.Title: Model Interpretability and Feature Importance
Elements.BodyText: Understanding which features drive model predictions is critical in engineering applications where decisions have physical and safety consequences.
Elements.Notes: Tree-based models provide built-in feature importances. SHAP values offer model-agnostic, consistent explanations and are increasingly used in high-stakes engineering decisions.

Items.Text: Feature importance scores from tree-based models rank input variables by their contribution to reducing prediction error.
Permutation importance measures how much model performance drops when a single feature is randomly shuffled.
Partial dependence plots show the marginal effect of one or two features on the predicted outcome.
Interpretable models build stakeholder trust and satisfy regulatory requirements in safety-critical engineering domains.

Topics.Title: Engineering Automation and Parameter Sweep Pipelines
Topics.Summary: Introduces strategies for automating repetitive engineering computations and systematically exploring parameter spaces using scripted pipelines.

Elements.Title: Why Automate Engineering Workflows?
Elements.BodyText: Automation replaces manual, error-prone repetition with reliable, reproducible scripts that can execute complex sequences of engineering tasks consistently.
Elements.Notes: Connect to real engineering contexts such as running finite element simulations across a range of load cases or generating performance curves for multiple design variants.

Items.Text: Automation reduces human error in tasks that involve many repetitive steps or large numbers of runs.
Scripts provide a complete record of the computation, supporting reproducibility and auditing.
Automated pipelines free engineers to focus on analysis and decision-making rather than manual execution.
Well-structured automation scripts are easier to maintain, update, and share across a team.

Elements.Title: Designing a Parameter Sweep
Elements.BodyText: A parameter sweep systematically varies one or more input parameters across a defined range and records the corresponding outputs to map system behavior.
Elements.Notes: Parameter sweeps are analogous to design-of-experiments approaches and can inform sensitivity analyses, optimization, and model validation.

Items.Text: Define the parameter space by specifying the variables to vary, their ranges, and the resolution or step size.
Use nested loops or vectorized operations to iterate over all parameter combinations efficiently.
Store results in structured formats such as DataFrames or CSV files for subsequent analysis.
Visualizing sweep results as heatmaps, contour plots, or line plots reveals how outputs respond to input changes.

Elements.Title: Integrating ML Models into Automation Pipelines
Elements.BodyText: Trained machine learning models can be embedded in automation pipelines to provide fast surrogate predictions in place of expensive simulations or experiments.
Elements.Notes: A trained surrogate model can evaluate thousands of parameter combinations in seconds, enabling rapid design space exploration that would be impractical with high-fidelity simulation.

Items.Text: Save trained models using joblib or pickle so they can be loaded and reused without retraining.
A pipeline that generates parameter combinations, queries the surrogate model, and records outputs can run fully automatically.
Validate surrogate model predictions against ground-truth simulation or experimental data before relying on them for design decisions.
Document model version, training data provenance, and validation results alongside the automation pipeline.

Elements.Title: Handling Large Result Sets
Elements.BodyText: Parameter sweeps can generate large volumes of output data that require efficient storage, querying, and summarization strategies.
Elements.Notes: Pandas DataFrames with indexed columns work well for moderate result sets. For very large sweeps, HDF5 or database storage may be warranted.

Items.Text: Use descriptive column names and include parameter values alongside results in every output row.
Pandas groupby and aggregation functions can summarize sweep results by parameter combinations.
Export summary statistics and visualizations automatically at the end of the sweep script.
Version the result files alongside the script in version control to maintain a clear record of which code produced which outputs.

Elements.Title: Scheduling and Running Long Jobs
Elements.BodyText: Engineering automation scripts that take significant time to run benefit from progress tracking, logging, and the ability to resume after interruption.
Elements.Notes: tqdm provides a simple progress bar for loops. Python's logging module is preferable to print statements for production automation scripts because it supports log levels and file output.

Items.Text: Add progress indicators to long-running loops so engineers can monitor execution and estimate completion time.
Implement checkpointing by saving intermediate results so that a script can resume from the last completed step after a failure.
Use structured logging to capture warnings, errors, and key milestones during automated runs.
Test automation scripts on a small parameter subset before launching full-scale runs to catch bugs early.

Topics.Title: Git and GitHub Version Control
Topics.Summary: Establishes foundational and collaborative version control practices using Git locally and GitHub as a remote repository platform for engineering code projects.

Elements.Title: Why Version Control Matters for Engineers
Elements.BodyText: Version control systems track every change made to a codebase, enabling engineers to review history, revert mistakes, and collaborate without overwriting each other's work.
Elements.Notes: Analogize to engineering change orders or revision-controlled drawings—version control is the software equivalent of a formal change management process.

Items.Text: Every commit captures a snapshot of the project at a specific point in time with an associated message explaining the change.
Version control eliminates the need for manual file naming conventions such as script_v2_final_FINAL.py.
The complete history of a project is preserved, enabling audit trails that satisfy engineering documentation requirements.
Multiple team members can work concurrently on the same project without destructive conflicts when using branching workflows.

Elements.Title: Core Git Concepts and Commands
Elements.BodyText: Git operates through a staging area and a local repository; understanding the add, commit, status, and log commands forms the foundation of daily Git use.
Elements.Notes: Walk through the basic cycle: make a change, git status to see it, git add to stage it, git commit to record it, git log to verify it.

Items.Text: git init creates a new local repository in the current directory.
git add stages changes, and git commit records them permanently with a descriptive message.
git status shows which files have been modified, staged, or are untracked.
git log displays the commit history, including hashes, authors, dates, and messages.

Elements.Title: Branching and Merging
Elements.BodyText: Branches allow engineers to develop features or experiments in isolation from the main codebase, and merging integrates completed work back into the main branch.
Elements.Notes: Feature branches prevent unstable or experimental code from breaking the main branch that other team members depend on.

Items.Text: git branch creates a new branch, and git checkout or git switch moves between branches.
Changes on a feature branch do not affect the main branch until they are explicitly merged.
git merge combines the history of one branch into another and may require resolving conflicts.
Descriptive branch names such as feature/add-regression-model communicate purpose clearly to collaborators.

Elements.Title: Working with GitHub Remote Repositories
Elements.BodyText: GitHub hosts remote copies of Git repositories, enabling cloud backup, sharing, and team collaboration through push and pull operations.
Elements.Notes: Distinguish between git push (local to remote) and git pull (remote to local). Explain that GitHub adds a web interface, issue tracking, and pull request workflows on top of the core Git protocol.

Items.Text: git remote add origin connects a local repository to a GitHub remote.
git push uploads local commits to the remote repository, making them accessible to collaborators.
git pull fetches and merges the latest remote changes into the current local branch.
SSH keys or personal access tokens are used to authenticate Git operations with GitHub securely.

Elements.Title: Pull Requests and Code Review
Elements.BodyText: Pull requests on GitHub are a formal mechanism for proposing changes, inviting peer review, and merging approved work into a shared branch.
Elements.Notes: Code review is a standard professional practice in software engineering that catches bugs, improves code quality, and transfers knowledge between team members.

Items.Text: A pull request compares a feature branch to a target branch and presents the differences for review.
Reviewers can comment on specific lines, request changes, or approve the pull request.
Continuous integration checks can run automated tests automatically when a pull request is opened.
Merging only approved pull requests into the main branch enforces quality control on shared codebases.

Elements.Title: Repository Hygiene and .gitignore
Elements.BodyText: Good repository hygiene ensures that only relevant source files are tracked, avoiding clutter from generated outputs, credentials, or environment-specific files.
Elements.Notes: A .gitignore file should be one of the first files created in any engineering project repository. Standard templates for Python projects are available at gitignore.io.

Items.Text: A .gitignore file lists file patterns that Git should not track, such as __pycache__, .env, and large data files.
Credentials and API keys must never be committed to a repository, even a private one.
Committing large binary files inflates repository size; use Git LFS or external storage for large datasets.
A clear README.md describing the project, setup instructions, and usage is essential for any shared repository.

Topics.Title: Responsible AI Integration in Engineering Practice
Topics.Summary: Consolidates the ethical, professional, and technical obligations engineers must uphold when using AI tools, including verification of AI-generated code and accountability for outcomes.

Elements.Title: The Role of AI Tools in Engineering Workflows
Elements.BodyText: AI coding assistants and generative tools can accelerate engineering software development, but they introduce new responsibilities around verification and professional judgment.
Elements.Notes: Examples include GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, and similar tools that generate code from natural language prompts. Emphasize that these tools produce plausible-sounding code that may contain subtle errors.

Items.Text: AI tools can generate boilerplate code, suggest algorithms, and explain unfamiliar library functions, increasing developer productivity.
Generated code reflects patterns in training data and may incorporate outdated practices or deprecated APIs.
Engineers remain responsible for every line of code submitted under their name, regardless of how it was generated.
AI tools do not understand engineering context, physical constraints, or safety requirements unless explicitly provided.

Elements.Title: Verification of AI-Generated Code
Elements.BodyText: Every piece of code produced by an AI assistant must be critically reviewed, tested, and validated before it is used in any engineering computation or product.
Elements.Notes: A structured verification checklist can include: Does the code do what it claims? Are the units correct? Does it handle edge cases? Have I tested it against a known result?

Items.Text: Read AI-generated code line by line and ensure you understand what each section does before executing it.
Test generated functions against known inputs and manually verified outputs, including boundary conditions.
Check that numerical methods, formulas, and constants embedded in generated code are correct and appropriately sourced.
Never deploy AI-generated code in safety-critical applications without independent review and validation testing.

Elements.Title: Recognizing Hallucinations and Errors
Elements.BodyText: AI language models can confidently produce incorrect code, fabricated function names, or physically unreasonable results—a phenomenon known as hallucination.
Elements.Notes: Common hallucination patterns include inventing library functions that do not exist, citing incorrect equation forms, and providing plausible but wrong numerical constants.

Items.Text: AI-generated code may reference functions or arguments that do not exist in the specified library version.
Physically unreasonable outputs such as negative absolute temperatures or efficiencies above 100% signal an error in the generated logic.
Cross-check any domain-specific formula or constant against an authoritative engineering reference before use.
Skepticism toward AI output is a professional competency, not a limitation of the tool.

Elements.Title: Professional Engineering Accountability
Elements.BodyText: Licensed and professional engineers bear legal and ethical responsibility for the work they produce and cannot transfer that responsibility to an AI tool.
Elements.Notes: Reference professional codes of conduct such as those from NSPE, IEEE, or ASCE, which place public safety above efficiency or convenience.

Items.Text: Professional engineering codes require that engineers act in the interest of public health, safety, and welfare above all else.
Using AI to generate code does not constitute due diligence if the engineer has not verified its correctness.
Documentation of verification steps protects the engineer professionally and supports quality assurance audits.
Employers and clients expect engineers to exercise independent judgment, even when AI tools are part of the workflow.

Elements.Title: Bias, Fairness, and Equity in Engineering ML Systems
Elements.BodyText: Machine learning models trained on historical data can inherit and amplify societal biases, leading to unfair or discriminatory outcomes in engineering systems that affect people.
Elements.Notes: Engineering applications such as predictive maintenance scheduling, resource allocation, and infrastructure prioritization can have disparate impacts if bias is not actively identified and mitigated.

Items.Text: Training data that underrepresents certain populations or conditions will produce models that perform poorly for those cases.
Engineers must audit model predictions across relevant demographic or geographic subgroups before deployment.
Fairness metrics such as equalized odds or demographic parity can quantify disparate model performance.
Bias mitigation is an ongoing process requiring monitoring after deployment, not a one-time pre-launch check.

Elements.Title: Transparent Documentation and Reproducibility
Elements.BodyText: Responsible AI practice requires that models, datasets, training procedures, and validation results be documented thoroughly so that others can understand, audit, and reproduce the work.
Elements.Notes: Model cards, introduced by Google, are a standardized format for documenting model intended use, performance metrics, and limitations. Datasheets for Datasets provide equivalent documentation for training data.

Items.Text: Document the source, collection method, preprocessing steps, and known limitations of every dataset used to train or evaluate a model.
Record model architecture, hyperparameters, training environment, and performance metrics in a model card or equivalent artifact.
Version control all training scripts, configuration files, and model artifacts to ensure any past result can be reproduced.
Share documentation with stakeholders so they can make informed decisions about whether and how to rely on the model.

Elements.Title: Integrating Responsible AI Habits into Daily Practice
Elements.BodyText: Responsible AI is not a separate checklist but a set of habits—verification, documentation, skepticism, and ethical reflection—that should be embedded in every engineering workflow.
Elements.Notes: Encourage learners to develop personal checklists or team protocols that make responsible AI practices routine rather than exceptional.

Items.Text: Make code review that includes AI-generated content a standard part of team pull request workflows.
Set aside time to test and validate any AI-assisted computation before it influences a design decision.
Raise concerns about AI-generated outputs that seem physically inconsistent or ethically problematic, even under time pressure.
Continuous learning about evolving AI capabilities and limitations is a professional obligation for practicing engineers.
